title: NSG Flow Log Analysis
spl:
index=azure sourcetype="mscs:azure:nsgflowlog" flowState="D"
| stats count by src, dest, dest_port, protocol
| sort -count | head 20
description: NSG Flow Logs provide Azure network-level visibility. Detects blocked traffic, anomalous patterns, and lateral movement within VNets.
value: NSG Flow Logs provide Azure network-level visibility. Detects blocked traffic, anomalous patterns, and lateral movement within VNets.
implementation: Enable NSG Flow Logs (Version 2) on all NSGs. Send to a storage account. Ingest via Splunk_TA_microsoft-cloudservices. Create dashboards for denied traffic and top talkers.
visualization: Table (top denied flows), Sankey diagram, Timechart, Map.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚ÄĘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_microsoft-cloudservices`.
‚ÄĘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=mscs:azure:nsgflowlog`.
‚ÄĘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Configure data collection
Enable NSG Flow Logs (Version 2) on all NSGs. Send to a storage account. Ingest via Splunk_TA_microsoft-cloudservices. Create dashboards for denied traffic and top talkers.

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=azure sourcetype="mscs:azure:nsgflowlog" flowState="D"
| stats count by src, dest, dest_port, protocol
| sort -count | head 20
```

Understanding this SPL

**NSG Flow Log Analysis** ‚ÄĒ NSG Flow Logs provide Azure network-level visibility. Detects blocked traffic, anomalous patterns, and lateral movement within VNets.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=mscs:azure:nsgflowlog`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_microsoft-cloudservices`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚ÄĒrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: azure; **sourcetype**: mscs:azure:nsgflowlog. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚ÄĘ Scopes the data: index=azure, sourcetype="mscs:azure:nsgflowlog". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚ÄĘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src, dest, dest_port, protocol** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚ÄĘ Orders rows with `sort` ‚ÄĒ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
‚ÄĘ Limits the number of rows with `head`.


Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (top denied flows), Sankey diagram, Timechart, Map.
grandmaExplanation: NSG Flow Logs provide Azure network-level visibility ‚ÄĒ so risky changes, runaway spending, and suspicious logins do not go unnoticed.
